name: Fragment / Incremental-Reveal Intent
summary: A finer-than-slide step-reveal: content within a single slide appears in ordered steps as the presenter advances, rather than all at once. Declares the reveal order (document order, or an explicit fragment index that reorders), the per-step transition (each step is its own View Transition without re-animating the whole slide), and reverse traversal on back-navigation (a step un-reveals). Composes the view + view-transition semantics and is a member of the advanceable-sequence family (#1179). The subtle part — and why this is its own contract — is the overloaded 'next': advancing must reveal the next fragment if one remains, else change slide.

order.description: The sequence steps are revealed in. source = document order (the default); index = an explicit per-fragment index that reorders reveal independent of DOM position (a later element revealing first).

traversal.description: Back-navigation behavior. bidirectional = reverse traversal un-reveals the last step (back-nav steps fragments backward before changing slide — the default for a deck); forward = steps only reveal, never un-reveal (back-nav jumps straight to the previous slide).

step.description: How each step animates. view-transition = each reveal is its own View Transition over just the affected element (no whole-slide re-animation); none = instant appearance (and the conformance default under prefers-reduced-motion).

description:
Step reveal within a slide

A slide often reveals its content in steps — a bullet at a time, a diagram building up — as the presenter advances. This is finer than slide-to-slide change: the slide stays, its pieces appear in order. The Fragment intent declares that order, the per-step transition, and what back-navigation does.

The overloaded 'next'

The load-bearing subtlety: a single 'advance' action must reveal the next fragment if one remains, otherwise change slide. Symmetrically, 'back' un-reveals the last fragment before stepping to the previous slide (under bidirectional traversal). This dual meaning is why fragments are a contract, not just a CSS animation — the navigation model and the reveal model are entangled.

Composition

  • View / View-Transition: each step composes the view + view-transition semantics, scoped to the revealed element so the rest of the slide does not re-animate.
  • Advanceable-Sequence: a member of the advanceable-sequence family (#1179) — fragments are the deck's added layer over the shared advance kernel.
  • Reduced motion: the step: none path is the conforming fallback under prefers-reduced-motion (the View Transitions gate the native API omits — folded into the a11y vector set #1195).
